purpose: White-box rules for normalizing benchmark inputs, validating intermediate tables, and making safe comparison judgements.
core_principles: Support two input formats now: raw clipboard text and xlsx files.
Normalize every input into an intermediate structured table before judgement.
Validate the normalized table before any comparison; stop if validation fails.
Support flexible header alignment instead of requiring perfect schema matches.
Missing or misaligned fields lower confidence; they do not cause runtime failure.
If information is insufficient, return insufficient information instead of making a claim.
Do not hallucinate missing values, hidden assumptions, or chip advantages.
Keep judgement logic white-box, editable, and auditable.

input_policy.format_policy: Both formats are first-class inputs and should use the same downstream normalization, validation, and judgement pipeline.

train_days_estimate: est. time to train in days

stop_conditions: all extracted rows missing key identifiers
numeric parsing fails on critical fields needed for the requested judgement
units are contradictory and cannot be resolved
training and inference metrics are mixed without enough labeling

comparison_policy.alignment_strategy: Use any overlapping canonical fields; do not require full alignment.
comparison_policy.missing_field_behavior: Treat missing values as unknown, reduce confidence, and explain the gap.
comparison_policy.mismatch_behavior: Surface mismatches explicitly and weaken the claim instead of blocking immediately.
comparison_policy.hard_block_behavior: If evidence is too weak for the requested judgement, return insufficient information.

harder_condition_assumptions: fewer chips is harder for total throughput claims
smaller batch size is harder for throughput
parallelism differences can affect comparability and should be surfaced

state_rules.better: Use only when throughput is higher and workload conditions are equal or harder with enough aligned evidence.
state_rules.tentative better: Use when throughput is higher but some important workload factors are easier or not fully aligned.
state_rules.similar: Use when compared throughput metrics are close and no strong advantage is supported.
state_rules.worse: Use when throughput is lower under comparable enough conditions.
state_rules.insufficient information: Use when key comparison evidence is missing, conflicting, or too weak.

anti_hallucination_policy.required_safe_message: Information is not enough to make a reliable judgement.
